heroOverview: Active rickets is a metabolic bone disease of children caused by defective mineralisation of growing bone and the epiphyseal growth plate, resulting from prolonged deficiency of calcium, phosphate, or vitamin D. Unlike osteomalacia (the adult equivalent), rickets affects the growing skeleton and produces distinctive skeletal deformities during the period of rapid growth. Globally, vitamin D deficiency rickets remains the most common form, affecting an estimated 1 billion people worldwide with higher prevalence in northern latitudes, darker-skinned populations, exclusively breastfed infants, and children with limited sun exposure. Clinical features include bowing of the legs, rachitic rosary (costochondral beading), craniotabes, delayed fontanelle closure, and growth retardation. The disease responds well to vitamin D and calcium supplementation in nutritional rickets, but underlying genetic causes require specific therapy. Without treatment, severe rickets causes permanent skeletal deformities, growth impairment, and increased fracture risk.

keyStats.prevalence: Vitamin D deficiency rickets: 1-5 per 1,000 children in high-risk populations; hypophosphataemic rickets: 1 in 20,000; total global burden estimated at 1 billion with vitamin D insufficiency
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence in children aged 6 months to 3 years; higher prevalence in darker-skinned children in northern latitudes, exclusively breastfed infants, and those with minimal sun exposure; equal sex distribution
keyStats.avgAge: Clinical rickets usually manifests between 6 months and 3 years of age; hypophosphataemic rickets may present later (1-5 years)
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 12-20 million cases of nutritional rickets globally; highest burden in South Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and the Middle East

definition: Active rickets (ICD-10: E55.0, active rickets) is a disorder of bone mineralisation occurring during childhood when the epiphyseal growth plates are open. The underlying pathophysiology involves inadequate calcium and phosphate availability for bone mineralisation, resulting from: (1) vitamin D deficiency (nutritional, malabsorptive, or hepatic/renal disease), (2) hypophosphataemia (renal phosphate wasting from genetic or acquired causes), or (3) calcium deficiency alone. The hallmark is the rachitic growth plate — widened, irregular, and unmineralised — visible on X-ray as cupping, fraying, and widening of the metaphysis. The clinical manifestations include skeletal deformities (bowed legs or knock-knees, wrist and ankle widening, rachitic rosary, pectus deformity), growth failure, craniotabes, delayed fontanelle closure, and muscle weakness. Active rickets requires treatment to prevent permanent deformity; once the growth plates close (in adolescence), residual deformities remain as osteomalacia in the adult skeleton. The disease is distinguished from disorders of bone formation (osteogenesis imperfecta, metabolic bone disease of prematurity) by the characteristic radiological and biochemical findings.

typesClassification.type: Nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets (most common)
typesClassification.description: Inadequate dietary vitamin D and insufficient sunlight exposure; common in breastfed infants with dark skin in northern latitudes, exclusively breastfed babies without supplementation, and children with limited outdoor activity.
Defective renal phosphate reabsorption causing phosphate depletion and defective mineralisation; X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets (PHEX gene mutations) is the most common genetic form.

typesClassification.type: Vitamin D-dependent rickets type I (calcitriol deficiency)
typesClassification.description: Autosomal recessive defect in renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase (CYP27B1), preventing conversion of 25-OH vitamin D to active 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D; requires calcitriol therapy.

typesClassification.type: Vitamin D-dependent rickets type II (hereditary vitamin D resistance)
typesClassification.description: Autosomal recessive mutation in the vitamin D receptor (VDR) causing target organ resistance to calcitriol; presents with early-onset severe rickets, alopecia, and high calcitriol levels.

typesClassification.type: Calcium-deficiency rickets (hypocalcaemic rickets without vitamin D deficiency)
typesClassification.description: Low dietary calcium intake in the context of adequate vitamin D causing rickets; described in areas with very high phytate diets (rural South Asia) and calcium-poor weaning foods.
Chronic kidney disease causes phosphate retention, hypocalcaemia, and impaired activation of vitamin D, producing rickets-like bone disease. Treatment requires phosphate binders and calcitriol.

primarySymptoms: Bowing of the legs (genu varum) or knock-knees (genu valgum) presenting after the child begins to stand and walk, typically noticed between 1 and 3 years of age.
Rachitic rosary — visible and palpable enlargement of the costochondral junctions of the ribs, producing a beaded appearance along the lower border of the ribcage.
Wrist and ankle widening from metaphyseal expansion at the growing ends of long bones.
Delayed closure of the anterior fontanelle (remains open beyond 18 months, normal closure 9-18 months).
Craniotabes — softening of the skull bones, particularly at the occiput, producing a ping-pong ball sensation on palpation.
Progressive growth retardation with short stature and reduced growth velocity.
Muscle weakness and hypotonia with a waddling gait; affected children may be late to walk.
Pectus carinatum (pigeon chest) or pectus excavatum from softened and deformed ribs.
Dental enamel hypoplasia and delayed eruption of permanent teeth.
Bone pain and tenderness, particularly in the legs, causing reluctance to bear weight and pain on handling.
earlyWarningSigns: Growth faltering or short stature in a child with limited sun exposure or dark skin
Visible bowing of the legs or other skeletal deformities after the child begins walking
Persistently open anterior fontanelle beyond 18 months of age
Rachitic rosary or chest deformity in a young child
Delayed tooth eruption or enamel defects in a child with growth concerns
Wrist widening or other metaphyseal changes on X-ray of a child with clinical signs
emergencySigns: Hypocalcaemic seizures or tetany presenting with seizures, perioral paraesthesia, carpopedal spasm, and laryngospasm — requires urgent IV calcium gluconate
Severe bone pain preventing weight-bearing or causing respiratory compromise from chest wall deformity
Pathological fractures through rachitic bone — requires orthopaedic assessment and appropriate immobilisation
Respiratory compromise from severe chest wall deformity (pectus excavatum with paradox) — requires specialist review
Severe growth failure with failure to thrive requiring nutritional assessment and supplementation

causes.cause: Vitamin D deficiency from inadequate intake and sun exposure
causes.description: Nutritional rickets results from insufficient dietary vitamin D (breast milk is low in vitamin D, 25-40 IU/L) and inadequate cutaneous synthesis from sunlight (UVB radiation converts 7-dehydrocholesterol to pre-vitamin D3 in skin). Dark skin pigmentation reduces UVB absorption by up to 90%, increasing risk in pigmented populations at higher latitudes. Seasonal variation in northern latitudes reduces cutaneous vitamin D synthesis for 4-6 months of the year.

X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets (XLH) is caused by PHEX gene mutations leading to elevated circulating FGF23, which suppresses renal phosphate reabsorption and reduces 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D production. The resulting hypophosphataemia impairs hydroxyapatite formation in bone. XLH accounts for approximately 80% of hereditary hypophosphataemic rickets.

causes.cause: Calcium deficiency from inadequate dietary intake
causes.description: Rickets can develop from calcium deficiency alone, even with normal vitamin D levels. This is particularly described in young children in rural South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa where weaning diets are based on phytate-rich cereals that inhibit calcium absorption, and dairy intake is low. The combination of low calcium, some degree of vitamin D insufficiency, and high phytate load produces rickets.

Conditions causing fat malabsorption (coeliac disease, cystic fibrosis, inflammatory bowel disease, biliary atresia, chronic pancreatitis) impair absorption of dietary vitamin D (a fat-soluble vitamin), leading to deficiency and rickets despite adequate vitamin D intake.

causes.cause: Chronic kidney disease and renal osteodystrophy
causes.description: CKD causes phosphate retention, reduced renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase activity (impairing vitamin D activation), hypocalcaemia, and secondary hyperparathyroidism. The net effect is defective bone mineralisation producing renal osteodystrophy with rickets-like changes in growing children.

riskFactors.description: Melanin reduces UVB penetration of skin by up to 90%, significantly reducing cutaneous vitamin D synthesis. Dark-skinned children in northern latitudes are at 5-10 fold higher risk of vitamin D deficiency rickets.

riskFactors.factor: Exclusive breastfeeding without vitamin D supplementation
riskFactors.description: Breast milk contains only 25-40 IU/L vitamin D, insufficient for infant requirements (400 IU/day). All exclusively breastfed infants should receive 400 IU/day vitamin D supplement from birth.

riskFactors.factor: Limited outdoor activity and covered clothing (cultural, winter)
riskFactors.description: Children with minimal sun exposure (indoor living, cultural dress covering most skin) cannot synthesise sufficient vitamin D. Immigrants from sunny countries to northern latitudes are at particularly high risk.
Preterm infants have reduced vitamin D stores at birth, impaired intestinal absorption of vitamin D, and higher vitamin D requirements for catch-up growth.
Fat malabsorption impairs vitamin D absorption; children with these conditions require higher vitamin D doses and monitoring.
X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets is inherited in an X-linked dominant pattern; affected mothers have a 50% chance of passing the condition to children of both sexes.

affectedDemographics: Peak incidence in children aged 6 months to 3 years, coinciding with the period of rapid growth and increased calcium and phosphate demands.
Higher prevalence in darker-skinned populations at northern latitudes (South Asian, African, Middle Eastern children in the UK, Canada, Scandinavia, Australia).
Exclusive breastfed infants without vitamin D supplementation are at elevated risk regardless of maternal vitamin D status.
Children in resource-limited settings with high-phytate diets and limited access to dairy or vitamin D supplementation.
Children with chronic kidney disease or malabsorption syndromes regardless of skin colour.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of active rickets requires clinical suspicion (skeletal deformities, growth failure), biochemical confirmation (abnormal calcium, phosphate, vitamin D, PTH, alkaline phosphatase), and radiological demonstration of rachitic changes at the growth plate. Initial work-up includes: serum calcium (may be normal, low, or very low in different types), phosphate (low in XLH and nutritional rickets; normal in vitamin D-deficient rickets with PTH compensation), 25-OH vitamin D (low in nutritional deficiency; may be normal in XLH and VDDR types I and II), PTH (elevated in vitamin D deficiency; normal or low in XLH), and alkaline phosphatase (markedly elevated in active rickets). Wrist X-ray (AP view) demonstrates the classic rachitic changes: metaphyseal cupping, fraying, and widening; epiphyseal plate irregularity; and delayed appearance of ossification centres. Differentiating nutritional rickets from hypophosphataemic rickets and vitamin D-dependent rickets requires the full biochemical panel. Genetic testing for PHEX (XLH), CYP27B1 (VDDR type I), and VDR (VDDR type II) confirms hereditary forms.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses vitamin D status; <30 nmol/L indicates deficiency, 30-50 nmol/L insufficiency; <10 nmol/L severe deficiency; guides treatment dose
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood test; fasting not required; results in 1-3 days

diagnosticTests.purpose: Low calcium in VDDR and hypocalcaemic rickets; low phosphate in XLH and nutritional rickets; albumin-corrected calcium accounts for low albumin
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood tests; results within 24-48 hours

diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated PTH in vitamin D deficiency rickets (secondary hyperparathyroidism); normal or low in XLH; helps distinguish rickets types
Markedly elevated in active rickets (bone-specific ALP); used to monitor response to treatment; falls as rickets heals
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood test; results within 24-48 hours

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates classic rachitic changes: metaphyseal cupping, fraying, and widening; widened growth plate; delayed epiphyseal ossification
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Simple X-ray; results immediate or within 24 hours

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes renal osteodystrophy; elevated creatinine and abnormal eGFR suggest CKD-related rickets
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood test with creatinine; results within 24 hours

diagnosticTests.test: Urine phosphate and tubular reabsorption of phosphate (TRP)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Low TRP (<85%) indicates renal phosphate wasting characteristic of XLH; distinguishes from nutritional rickets where TRP is normal or elevated
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Timed urine collection; results in 1-3 days

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms hereditary forms (XLH, VDDR type I and II); cascade testing for family members; guides specific therapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood for DNA; 3-4 weeks for results

treatmentOverview: Treatment of active rickets is type-specific. Nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets is treated with vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) 2,000-4,000 IU daily orally for 8-12 weeks, followed by a maintenance dose of 400-600 IU daily, combined with calcium supplementation (500-1,000 mg/day of elemental calcium from diet or supplements). Stoss therapy (single oral dose of 200,000-600,000 IU vitamin D) is an alternative with comparable efficacy and better adherence. Hypophosphataemic rickets is treated with oral phosphate supplements (Phosphate Sandoz) divided into 4-5 doses daily, combined with active vitamin D (calcitriol 20-40 ng/kg/day) and, in growing children, burosumab (anti-FGF23 monoclonal antibody) 1-2 mg/kg subcutaneously every 2 weeks. Vitamin D-dependent rickets type I requires calcitriol (0.5-2 µg/day); type II requires high-dose calcitriol and calcium supplementation as the VDR is unresponsive to normal doses. Orthopaedic intervention (bracing, corrective osteotomy) is reserved for persistent or severe deformities after metabolic control. Sunlight exposure (30-60 minutes daily with face and arms exposed) is an important adjunct for nutritional rickets.

medicalTreatments.name: Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) 2,000-4,000 IU daily orally for 8-12 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Standard treatment for nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets; cholecalciferol is the preferred form. Oral administration with calcium supplements for 8-12 weeks achieves biochemical cure and radiological healing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Biochemical cure (normalisation of calcium, phosphate, ALP, PTH) within 4-8 weeks; radiological healing within 3-6 months; clinical improvement in symptoms and growth within weeks

medicalTreatments.name: Stoss therapy: single oral dose of vitamin D 200,000-600,000 IU
medicalTreatments.description: Single high-dose vitamin D therapy with equivalent efficacy to daily supplementation; better adherence; standard in some public health programmes for nutritional rickets.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent biochemical and radiological cure rates to daily therapy; calcium co-administration recommended; no evidence of toxicity with single dose

medicalTreatments.name: Calcium supplementation 500-1,000 mg elemental calcium daily
medicalTreatments.description: Essential adjunct to vitamin D therapy in nutritional rickets; calcium-rich diet (dairy, fortified foods) plus supplements; prevents hypocalcaemia during bone healing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents hypocalcaemia during rapid mineralisation of rachitic bone; improves healing rate when combined with vitamin D

medicalTreatments.name: Burosumab (anti-FGF23 monoclonal antibody) 1-2 mg/kg subcutaneously every 2 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Monoclonal antibody binding circulating FGF23; approved for XLH and other FGF23-mediated phosphate disorders. Neutralises the phosphaturic effect of FGF23.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Raises serum phosphate, lowers ALP, improves healing of rickets on X-ray, improves growth and reduces deformities in XLH; superior to conventional phosphate-calcitriol in head-to-head trials

medicalTreatments.name: Oral phosphate supplements (Phosphate Sandoz or equivalent) 20-60 mg/kg/day elemental phosphorus divided 4-5 doses
medicalTreatments.description: For XLH and renal phosphate wasting; replaces urinary phosphate losses; must be combined with active vitamin D (calcitriol) to prevent secondary hyperparathyroidism.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Raises serum phosphate and promotes mineralisation; requires careful monitoring and dose titration to avoid hyperparathyroidism and nephrocalcinosis

medicalTreatments.name: Calcitriol (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D) 0.5-2 µg/day orally
medicalTreatments.description: Active vitamin D metabolite for VDDR type I (renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency) and as adjunct to phosphate therapy in XLH; bypasses the enzymatic block.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects the biochemical defect in VDDR type I; raises serum calcium and suppresses PTH; must be carefully dosed to avoid hypercalcaemia

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose calcitriol with IV calcium for VDDR type II (severe)
medicalTreatments.description: VDR resistance requires very high doses of calcitriol (µg/day) and calcium supplementation; some cases require IV calcium infusions to maintain normal serum calcium.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Partial correction of mineralisation defect; severe cases may not fully respond; lifelong high-dose therapy required

medicalTreatments.name: Sunlight exposure 30-60 minutes daily with face and arms exposed
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunct treatment for nutritional rickets; UVB radiation stimulates cutaneous vitamin D synthesis; most effective in summer months at northern latitudes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Supports endogenous vitamin D production; complements oral therapy; must be balanced against skin cancer risk (sun protection in high-UV environments)

surgicalOptions.name: Corrective osteotomy for persistent limb deformity after metabolic control
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical correction of bowing (genu varum/valgum) after biochemical healing and growth plate closure; realigns the limb and improves function.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful realignment in 80-90%; performed after biochemical cure and growth plate maturity to prevent recurrence

surgicalOptions.name: Bracing (KAFO knee-ankle-foot orthosis) for progressive bowing in young children
surgicalOptions.description: Conservative management of limb deformity in growing children with XLH or nutritional rickets; delays or prevents need for osteotomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Prevents progression of bowing in 60-70% of children; allows normal activity and may improve alignment over time

surgicalOptions.name: Epiphysiodesis for progressive genu valgum or varum deformity
surgicalOptions.description: Guided growth procedure to correct angular limb deformity by arresting growth on the convex side of the deformity; for growing children.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Correction of angular deformity in 70-80% of growing children; less invasive than osteotomy

alternativeTreatments.name: Fortified foods (vitamin D-fortified milk, cereal, oil)
alternativeTreatments.description: Public health strategy for prevention of nutritional rickets; vitamin D fortification of staple foods (milk, flour, cooking oil) at population level reduces incidence.

alternativeTreatments.name: Maternal vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy and lactation
alternativeTreatments.description: Prenatal vitamin D supplementation (1,000-2,000 IU/day) improves maternal vitamin D status and reduces risk of neonatal hypocalcaemia and rickets in the first 6 months.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary calcium enrichment in high-phytate diet communities
alternativeTreatments.description: Addition of calcium-rich foods or calcium supplements to weaning diets in calcium-deficient populations to prevent calcium-deficiency rickets.

whySeeSpecialist: Active rickets requires specialist assessment by a paediatric endocrinologist or metabolic bone disease specialist to confirm the type of rickets, initiate appropriate treatment, and monitor for complications. Accurate biochemical and genetic diagnosis is essential because treatment differs dramatically between types. Orthopaedic input is needed for skeletal deformities.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a paediatric endocrinology or metabolic bone disease centre with expertise in rickets types, access to genetic testing, and experience with burosumab therapy for XLH. Ask about their experience with all rickets types, their approach to monitoring, and their orthopaedic collaboration.
hospitalCriteria: Paediatric endocrinology service with rickets and metabolic bone disease expertise
Genetic testing capability for hereditary rickets (PHEX, CYP27B1, VDR)
Biochemistry laboratory with calcium, phosphate, vitamin D, PTH, and ALP assays
Radiology with expertise in skeletal dysplasias and rickets X-ray reporting
Orthopaedics with paediatric limb reconstruction experience
keyFacilities: Genetic testing laboratory or reference laboratory access
Nutritional services with dietetics for calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Burosumab prescribing and monitoring service for XLH
preventionStrategies: Universal vitamin D supplementation for all infants and children: 400 IU/day for breastfed infants, 600-1,000 IU/day for older children, particularly in high-risk groups.
Vitamin D supplementation for pregnant and lactating women (1,000-2,000 IU/day) to ensure adequate maternal vitamin D status.
Food fortification with vitamin D (milk, flour, cooking oil) in populations at risk.
Public health education about sun exposure (30-60 minutes daily with face and arms exposed, balanced against skin cancer risk) and dietary calcium.
Screening of high-risk children (dark skin, exclusively breastfed, limited sun exposure, family history of XLH) with serum 25-OH vitamin D and ALP.
Early identification of rickets in primary care through growth monitoring and skeletal examination.
lifestyleModifications: Ensure vitamin D supplementation 400-1,000 IU daily for all children, especially breastfed infants and darker-skinned children at northern latitudes.

Encourage safe sun exposure: 30-60 minutes daily with face and arms exposed for light-skinned children; longer for darker-skinned children. Avoid excessive sun exposure and use sun protection in high-UV environments.
Dietary calcium intake of 500-1,000 mg/day through dairy products, fortified foods, or supplements for children at risk.
Regular monitoring of growth (height and weight) and skeletal examination for early detection of rickets.
Physical activity to support bone and muscle development; swimming is excellent for children with rickets as it is low-impact.

recommended: Calcium-rich diet: 500-1,000 mg/day from dairy products (milk, cheese, yoghurt), fortified plant milks, sardines with bones, leafy greens
Vitamin D from fortified foods (milk, cereals, orange juice), egg yolks, fatty fish (salmon, mackerel), and supplements
Protein-rich diet for growth and bone health
Adequate phosphorus from dairy, meat, fish, and legumes (especially for XLH patients on phosphate supplements)
avoid: Phytate-rich foods (unrefined cereals, legumes) in excess without calcium co-intake (phytate inhibits calcium absorption)
Excessive phosphate from carbonated drinks and processed foods (unhealthy for bone and exacerbates hyperphosphataemia in XLH)
Raw high-phytate diets without calcium supplementation in high-risk populations

exerciseGuidelines: Regular weight-bearing activity supports bone mineralisation and muscle strength. Swimming is ideal as it is non-weight-bearing yet builds muscle. Avoid high-impact sports if fractures are a concern or if deformities cause instability. Physical therapy supports gait and motor development.
dailyManagement: Administer vitamin D and calcium supplements daily as prescribed; do not miss doses.
For XLH patients: administer phosphate and calcitriol on schedule in divided doses; report nausea or abdominal discomfort from phosphate.
For XLH patients on burosumab: subcutaneous injection every 2 weeks; monitor for injection site reactions.
Attend all follow-up appointments for biochemistry and X-ray monitoring.
Ensure adequate sun exposure daily (face and arms, 30-60 minutes); use sun protection after vitamin D status has normalised.
prognosis: With early diagnosis and appropriate treatment, nutritional rickets heals completely: biochemical markers normalise within 4-8 weeks, radiological healing occurs within 3-6 months, and skeletal deformities improve or resolve in growing children. Long-term outcomes are excellent with no residual disability for most children who receive adequate treatment during the growth period. Delay in treatment leads to permanent skeletal deformities (bowed legs, chest wall deformity, short stature) that may require orthopaedic correction. Hypophosphataemic rickets requires lifelong phosphate and calcitriol therapy (or burosumab); outcome depends on compliance and monitoring for complications (nephrocalcinosis, hyperparathyroidism). VDDR types I and II require lifelong active vitamin D therapy; type II may be partially or completely resistant to calcitriol.
recoveryTimeline: Clinical improvement (reduced bone pain, improved gait) occurs within 2-4 weeks of starting treatment. Biochemical markers (calcium, phosphate, ALP, PTH) begin to normalise within 4-8 weeks. Radiological healing (visible on wrist X-ray) is evident by 3-6 months. Full radiological healing may take 6-12 months in severe cases. After biochemical cure, maintenance vitamin D and calcium are continued indefinitely. Growth improvement may continue for 1-2 years after biochemical cure. Residual deformities that fail to remodel require orthopaedic intervention after growth plate closure.
complications: Permanent skeletal deformities (bowed legs, knock-knees, chest wall deformity) from untreated or delayed treatment — requires orthopaedic correction after growth.
Growth failure and short stature from chronic rickets — improved with early treatment; some height deficit may persist.
Hypocalcaemic seizures and tetany from severe vitamin D deficiency — prevented with vitamin D supplementation; acute episodes require IV calcium.
Nephrocalcinosis from over-treatment with vitamin D and calcium or from phosphate therapy — monitored with annual renal ultrasound.
Secondary hyperparathyroidism from excessive phosphate therapy without adequate calcitriol — monitored with PTH and adjusted accordingly.
Pathological fractures through rachitic bone — prevented with early treatment; fractures treated with appropriate immobilisation.
Dental enamel hypoplasia and increased dental caries — preventive dental care and fluoride supplementation.
Impaired respiratory function from severe chest wall deformity (pectus excavatum) — rare; requires specialist respiratory review.

supportResources.name: The MAGIC Foundation — information on growth disorders including rickets
supportResources.description: US organization providing information and support for children with growth disorders including nutritional and genetic rickets.

supportResources.name: National Osteoporosis Society (UK) — vitamin D deficiency resources
supportResources.description: UK charity providing information on bone health, vitamin D deficiency, and bone disease in children.

supportResources.name: CLF (Children's Liver Disease Foundation) — for biliary atresia and fat malabsorption rickets
supportResources.description: UK charity supporting children with liver disease including rickets from fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption.

supportResources.name: Rare Bone Disease Alliance / XLH Alliance
supportResources.description: Patient organization specifically for X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets, providing information, advocacy, and research updates on XLH and burosumab.

confusedWithConditions.name: Osteogenesis Imperfecta
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: OI is a collagen defect causing brittle bones and blue sclerae; radiological shows thin bones, fractures, and wormian bones but not the metaphyseal cupping and fraying of rickets. Biochemical profile is normal in OI.

confusedWithConditions.name: Metabolic Bone Disease of Prematurity
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Occurs in preterm infants with low mineral intake; presents with fractures and demineralisation but distinct from rickets. Occurs in first 6 months; treated with increased calcium and phosphate.

confusedWithConditions.name: Renal Osteodystrophy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Secondary to CKD; phosphate retention and impaired vitamin D activation cause bone disease resembling rickets. Distinguished by abnormal renal function, elevated phosphate, and low calcium.

confusedWithConditions.name: Metaphyseal Chondrodysplasia (Jansen type)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Genetic skeletal dysplasia causing metaphyseal abnormalities resembling rickets; biochemical tests are normal. Radiology and genetic testing distinguish the two.

relatedConditions.name: Osteomalacia (Adult Rickets)
relatedConditions.relevance: Osteomalacia is the adult equivalent of rickets — impaired mineralisation of mature bone after growth plate closure. It has the same metabolic causes (vitamin D deficiency, phosphate deficiency) but different clinical presentation (pseudofractures, diffuse bone pain) without the growth plate abnormalities of rickets.

relatedConditions.name: Hypophosphatasia
relatedConditions.relevance: A genetic bone disease caused by ALPL mutations producing low alkaline phosphatase; presents with rickets-like features and low ALP (opposite to rickets). The biochemical profile (low ALP) distinguishes it from nutritional and other rickets.

relatedConditions.name: X-Linked Hypophosphataemic Rickets (XLH)
relatedConditions.relevance: XLH is the most common genetic form of rickets, caused by PHEX mutations leading to FGF23 excess and renal phosphate wasting. Treatment differs from nutritional rickets (phosphate + calcitriol or burosumab).

faqs.question: What is active rickets?
faqs.answer: Active rickets is a childhood bone disease caused by defective mineralisation of growing bone and the epiphyseal growth plate, leading to widened, irregular metaphyses and skeletal deformities. It results from prolonged calcium, phosphate, or vitamin D deficiency.

faqs.question: What causes rickets in children?
faqs.answer: Rickets is caused by inadequate vitamin D, calcium, or phosphate for bone mineralisation. Most cases are from vitamin D deficiency (limited sun exposure, dark skin, exclusive breastfeeding without supplementation). Other causes include hypophosphataemia (XLH) and genetic defects in vitamin D metabolism.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of rickets?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include bowed legs or knock-knees, rachitic rosary (rib beading), widening of wrists and ankles, delayed fontanelle closure, craniotabes, growth failure, muscle weakness, and bone pain. Infants may be irritable and slow to reach motor milestones.

faqs.question: How is rickets diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis involves clinical examination (skeletal deformities), blood tests (low 25-OH vitamin D, low calcium, low phosphate, high ALP, high PTH), and wrist X-ray showing metaphyseal cupping, fraying, and widening.

faqs.question: How is rickets treated?
faqs.answer: Nutritional rickets is treated with vitamin D (2,000-4,000 IU daily for 8-12 weeks) and calcium supplements. Hypophosphataemic rickets requires oral phosphate and calcitriol, or burosumab injection. Genetic forms require specific lifelong therapy.

faqs.question: Can rickets be cured?
faqs.answer: Nutritional rickets can be cured completely with early treatment; biochemical markers normalise within weeks and skeletal deformities improve in growing children. Genetic forms (XLH, VDDR) require lifelong management but are well-controlled with appropriate therapy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between rickets and osteomalacia?
faqs.answer: Rickets affects children with open growth plates; osteomalacia affects adults with closed growth plates. Both result from impaired bone mineralisation. In rickets, the growth plate is widened and deformed; in osteomalacia, pseudofractures (Looser zones) appear in mature bone.

faqs.question: Does breastfeeding cause rickets?
faqs.answer: Breast milk alone does not cause rickets if the infant receives 400 IU/day vitamin D supplement from birth. Breast milk is low in vitamin D (25-40 IU/L), but supplemented breastfed infants are protected. All exclusively breastfed infants should receive vitamin D supplementation.

faqs.question: How much vitamin D do children need to prevent rickets?
faqs.answer: Infants and children need 400-600 IU of vitamin D daily. High-risk children (dark skin, limited sun exposure, exclusive breastfeeding) may need 600-1,000 IU daily. This can come from supplements, fortified foods, or safe sun exposure.

faqs.question: What is rachitic rosary?
faqs.answer: Rachitic rosary is the visible and palpable enlargement of the costochondral junctions (where the ribs meet the cartilage) along the lower border of the ribcage. It is a characteristic sign of active rickets caused by metaphyseal expansion at the growing ends of the ribs.

faqs.question: Can rickets cause permanent deformities?
faqs.answer: Yes. Delayed or untreated rickets causes permanent skeletal deformities including bowed legs, knock-knees, chest wall deformities, and short stature. Early treatment during growth allows deformities to remodel and improves outcome.

faqs.question: Is rickets hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most cases of nutritional rickets are not hereditary. However, X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets (XLH) is inherited (X-linked dominant) and vitamin D-dependent rickets types I and II are autosomal recessive. These genetic forms require specific lifelong treatment.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy for children with rickets?
faqs.answer: With appropriate treatment, life expectancy is normal for children with rickets. Nutritional rickets is fully curable. Even XLH and VDDR, which require lifelong management, have normal life expectancy with good treatment adherence.

faqs.question: Is rickets painful?
faqs.answer: Yes. Active rickets causes bone pain and tenderness, particularly in the legs. Children are often reluctant to bear weight and may have a waddling gait. Pain improves with effective treatment.

faqs.question: What does wrist X-ray show in rickets?
faqs.answer: Wrist X-ray in rickets shows metaphyseal cupping (concave metaphyseal surface), fraying (irregular metaphyseal edge), and widening of the growth plate. The ossification centres appear delayed. These changes are the radiographic hallmark of active rickets.

faqs.question: How quickly does rickets heal with treatment?
faqs.answer: Clinical improvement (reduced pain, improved gait) occurs within 2-4 weeks. Biochemical markers normalise within 4-8 weeks. Radiological healing appears by 3-6 months. Most children fully recover with early treatment.

faqs.question: Does rickets affect teeth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Rickets causes dental enamel hypoplasia and delayed eruption of both primary and permanent teeth. Children with rickets require preventive dental care and regular dental review.

faqs.question: What is burosumab for rickets?
faqs.answer: Burosumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks FGF23, the hormone causing phosphate wasting in XLH. It is given by subcutaneous injection every 2 weeks and effectively treats hypophosphataemic rickets, improving phosphate levels, healing rickets on X-ray, and improving growth.

faqs.question: Can adults get rickets?
faqs.answer: Adults cannot develop rickets because their growth plates have closed. However, adults with chronic vitamin D deficiency can develop osteomalacia, the adult equivalent with similar biochemical causes but pseudofractures rather than growth plate abnormalities.

faqs.question: How common is rickets today?
faqs.answer: Nutritional rickets remains common in high-risk populations: 1-5 per 1,000 children in South Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and among dark-skinned children in northern latitudes. Global burden is estimated at 12-20 million cases of active rickets.
